label: Divrei HaYamim Bet (2 Chronicles) — Part 1 — Chapters 1–18
summary_plain: Divrei HaYamim Bet opens with Shelomoh asking YHWH for wisdom, not riches or power. YHWH grants him wisdom and adds riches and honor. Shelomoh builds the temple — cedar from Levanon, gold overlays, the great basin called the Sea — and dedicates it with prayer and sacrifice. The glory of YHWH fills the house as a cloud. The Queen of Sheva comes to test Shelomoh with hard questions and is overwhelmed by his wisdom. She says: Happy are your servants who stand before you and hear your wisdom. After Shelomoh's death, the kingdom divides. Rechav'am rules Yehudah. The Chronicler traces the southern kingdom's rulers through reforms and failures.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from DSS fragments and the Orit Ge'ez. The Queen of Sheva narrative (chapter 9) is critical. Sheva (Saba) was an ancient kingdom spanning the southern Arabian Peninsula and the Horn of Africa — modern Yemen, Eritrea, Ethiopia, and Djibouti. The Ethiopian tradition (Kebra Nagast) identifies the Queen of Sheva as Makeda and records that she bore Shelomoh a son, Menelik I, founder of the Solomonic dynasty of Ethiopia. This is not folklore — the Solomonic dynasty ruled Ethiopia from approximately 1270 to 1974 CE. The Aksumite Empire (c. 100-940 CE) controlled both sides of the Red Sea, including Saba. The trade corridor between Yisra'EL and Sheva was the African-Arabian spice and incense route — a continuous commercial and cultural connection. The Orit Ge'ez preserves the Sheva connection as living tradition. The Great Eraser separates Ethiopia from Yisra'EL by treating the Red Sea as a continental boundary when it is actually a narrow waterway that connected, not divided, the African and Arabian shores.

key_terms.definition: The queen who visited Shelomoh to test him with hard questions (chapter 9). Sheva (Saba) spanned southern Arabia and the Horn of Africa. The Ethiopian tradition identifies her as Makeda, mother of Menelik I. Anglicized as the Queen of Sheba.

The house Shelomoh built for YHWH in Yerushalayim. The Chronicler devotes chapters 2-7 to its construction and dedication. When the glory of YHWH filled it as a cloud, the priests could not stand to minister.

2 Chronicles 7:1-2: 'The glory of YHWH filled the house. The priests could not enter the house of YHWH because the glory of YHWH filled it.' The kavod is YHWH's manifest presence — visible, tangible, overwhelming.

fill_blank.prompt: Give me now wisdom and ______, that I may go out and come in before this people.
fill_blank.source_quote: Give me now wisdom and knowledge, that I may go out and come in before this people, for who can govern this people of yours, which is so great?

fill_blank.prompt: The Queen of Sheva said to Shelomoh: The report was true that I heard in my own land of your words and of your ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: She said to the king: The report was true that I heard in my own land of your words and of your wisdom.

fill_blank.prompt: When Shelomoh finished his prayer, fire came down from heaven and consumed the burnt offering, and the ______ of YHWH filled the house.
fill_blank.source_quote: As soon as Shelomoh finished his prayer, fire came down from heaven and consumed the burnt offering and the sacrifices, and the glory of YHWH filled the house.

multiple_choice.question: What did Shelomoh ask YHWH for?
options: Wisdom and knowledge to govern YHWH's people
multiple_choice.source_quote: Give me now wisdom and knowledge, that I may go out and come in before this people.

multiple_choice.question: What did the Queen of Sheva do when she visited Shelomoh?
options: She tested him with hard questions and was overwhelmed by his wisdom — she said the report did not tell half of it
She refused to enter the palace
multiple_choice.source_quote: When the Queen of Sheva had seen the wisdom of Shelomoh... there was no more breath in her. She said: The report was true... but the half of it was not told to me. Your wisdom surpasses the report that I heard.

multiple_choice.question: What happened when Shelomoh dedicated the temple?
options: Fire came down from heaven and the glory of YHWH filled the house — the priests could not enter
multiple_choice.source_quote: Fire came down from heaven and consumed the burnt offering and the sacrifices, and the glory of YHWH filled the house. And the priests could not enter the house of YHWH, because the glory of YHWH filled the house.

faq.question: What does the Queen of Sheva narrative reveal about the African-Arabian connection to Yisra'EL?
faq.answer: The Queen of Sheva came from Saba — an ancient kingdom that spanned the southern Arabian Peninsula and the Horn of Africa (modern Yemen, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Djibouti). The Red Sea did not divide these territories — it connected them. The Aksumite Empire (c. 100-940 CE) controlled both shores. The Ethiopian tradition (Kebra Nagast) identifies the Queen as Makeda and records that she bore Shelomoh a son, Menelik I, who founded the Solomonic dynasty. That dynasty ruled Ethiopia from approximately 1270 to 1974 CE — the longest continuous monarchical line in recorded history. The Orit Ge'ez was preserved by the community descended from this connection. The Great Eraser separates Ethiopia from Yisra'EL — treating the African Israelite tradition as derivative or legendary — when the textual, genetic, and archaeological evidence points to continuous connection across the African-Arabian trade corridor. The spice route from Sheva to Yerushalayim was the commercial backbone of Shelomoh's wealth.

faq.question: Why does Shelomoh ask for wisdom and not power?
faq.answer: 2 Chronicles 1:10: 'Give me now wisdom and knowledge, that I may go out and come in before this people.' YHWH responds: because you asked for wisdom and not riches, power, or the life of your enemies, I give you wisdom and add riches and honor as well (1:11-12). This is the covenant framework: the covenant leader's first obligation is wisdom — the capacity to govern YHWH's people according to YHWH's instruction. Not military force. Not economic extraction. Not personal glory. Wisdom. The same Chokmah (Wisdom) personified in Mishlei 1-9, the same Torah-wisdom celebrated in Psalm 119. Every Great Eraser system elevates institutional power over wisdom — credentialed authority over direct knowledge. Shelomoh's request says: wisdom is the prerequisite. Everything else follows.

faq.question: What is the Aksumite connection to the Israelite covenant?
faq.answer: The Aksumite Empire, centered in modern-day Eritrea and northern Ethiopia, controlled both sides of the Red Sea from approximately 100 to 940 CE. Aksum was one of the four great world powers of late antiquity — alongside Rome, Persia, and China. The Aksumite script (Ge'ez) preserved the Orit — the Torah, the prophets, Chanokh, Yovelim, and other texts that the Masoretic canon later excluded. The Beta Israel maintained covenant practice — Shabbat, kashrut, pilgrimage, sacrifice — in Ethiopia for millennia before European contact. The Queen of Sheva narrative in 2 Chronicles 9 places the connection at the time of Shelomoh — the 10th century BCE. The Aksumite civilization that later preserved the Orit is the historical continuation of that Sheva-Yisra'EL corridor. The Great Eraser treats this entire tradition as marginal or folkloric, when it is in fact the oldest continuous preservation of the broader Israelite scriptural canon.
